step.title: Step 51
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 183, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 182 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After RIGHT x28, something significant changed:
- The center blue square frame has been split! The charcoal frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39 is now split into two parts:
  - id=8: charcoal frame at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (right half)
  - The left half (cols 32-35) is now missing or changed
- The player is at rows 36-39, cols 28-31 (green at cols 28-30, white at col 31)
- The blue centers at cols 33-34 and 37-38 are still there
- The frame's left half seems to have been "consumed" or changed by the player entering it
Wait, let me look more carefully:
- id=5: N (green) at rows 36-39, cols 28-30 (player body)
- id=6: W (white) at rows 36-39, col 31 (white extension)
- id=8: c (charcoal) at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (right half of frame)
- id=9: b (blue) at rows 37-38, cols 33-34 (left blue center)
- id=10: b (blue) at rows 37-38, cols 37-38 (right blue center)
So the center blue square frame was originally at rows 36-39, cols 32-39. After the player moved RIGHT x28, the player is at cols 28-31. The frame's left half (cols 32-35) is now missing, and the frame's right half (cols 36-39) is still there. The blue centers are still there.
This suggests that when the player moved through the frame, the frame's left half was "consumed" or changed. The player is now at cols 28-31, which is to the LEFT of the frame's original position (cols 32-39).
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The player was at cols 4-7, and I moved RIGHT x28. The player should be at cols 32-35, not cols 28-31. Let me recalculate:
- Player at cols 4-7 (4 cells wide)
- RIGHT x28: player moves 28 cells RIGHT
- New position: cols 4+28=32 to cols 7+28=35
But the segmentation shows the player at cols 28-31. This suggests that the player didn't move all 28 cells RIGHT. Maybe the player hit something and stopped.
Actually, looking at the segmentation more carefully:
- id=5: N (green) at rows 36-39, cols 28-30 (3 cells wide, not 4)
- id=6: W (white) at rows 36-39, col 31 (1 cell wide)
So the player is at cols 28-31, with the green body at cols 28-30 and the white extension at col 31. The player is 4 cells wide (cols 28-31), which is correct.
But the player was at cols 4-7, and I moved RIGHT x28. The player should be at cols 32-35. But the player is at cols 28-31. This suggests that the player only moved 24 cells RIGHT, not 28.
Wait, maybe the player hit the frame and stopped. The frame was at cols 32-39. If the player was at cols 4-7 and moved RIGHT, the player would eventually reach cols 28-31 (just before the frame at cols 32-39). When the player tried to move further RIGHT, it hit the frame and stopped.
But the segmentation shows that the frame's left half (cols 32-35) is now missing. This suggests that the player "consumed" or changed the frame's left half when it moved through it.
Actually, I think what happened is:
1. The player was at cols 4-7.
2. The player moved RIGHT x28.
3. The player moved through the frame's left half (cols 32-35), consuming it.
4. The player stopped at cols 28-31, which is just before the frame's right half (cols 36-39).
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. If the player consumed the frame's left half, the player should be at cols 32-35 or further RIGHT. But the player is at cols 28-31, which is to the LEFT of the frame's original position.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the player didn't move all 28 cells RIGHT. Maybe the player only moved 24 cells RIGHT, stopping at cols 28-31. The frame's left half (cols 32-35) was "consumed" or changed because the player was adjacent to it.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the player's white extension "consumes" or changes objects when it's adjacent to them. The white extension is at col 31, pointing RIGHT. The frame's left half (cols 32-35) was adjacent to the white extension, so it was consumed.
But the frame's right half (cols 36-39) is still there. This suggests that the white extension only consumed the frame's left half, not the entire frame.
Let me try pressing SPACE to see if it does something now that the player is adjacent to the frame's right half.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 36-39, cols 28-31 (green at 28-30, white at 31)
# Frame right half at rows 36-39, cols 36-39
# Blue centers at cols 33-34 and 37-38
# The frame's left half (cols 32-35) was consumed when the player moved through it
# Try pressing SPACE now that the player is adjacent to the frame

result = action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Score:", result.get('score'))
print("Done:", result.get('done'))
print("Level completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
print("No impact:", result.get('no_impact'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
